en: Xuanshen (Scrophularia root). Taste bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. Indicated for cold-heat accumulations in the abdomen, and women's residual ailments after childbirth or nursing. Tonifies kidney qi and brightens the eyes. Also called Chongtai. Grows in river valleys.

Kushen (Sophora flavescens root). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for bound qi in the heart and abdomen, hernia-like masses and accumulations, jaundice, dribbling residual urination; expels water, removes carbuncles and swelling. Tonifies the middle, brightens the eyes, stops tearing. Also called Shuihuai, also called Kushi. Grows in mountain valleys and fields.

Gouji ("dog spine" root, Cibotium rhizome). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for stiffness of the lower back, slackness or spasm of the joints, generalized painful obstruction (bi), and cold-damp knee pain. Quite beneficial for the elderly. Also called Baizhi. Grows in river valleys.

Bixie (Dioscorea root). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for stiff pain of the lower back, and wind-cold-damp painful obstruction (bi) of the bones and joints. For malignant sores that will not heal, and heat qi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tongcao (Akebia/Tetrapanax stem). Taste pungent, neutral. Expels harmful parasites, removes cold-heat of the spleen and stomach, frees the nine orifices, the blood vessels, and the joints, and improves memory. Also called Fuzhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Qumai (Dianthus, fringed pink). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for obstruction and blockage disorders of all kinds, urinary stoppage; expels splinters, resolves carbuncles and swelling, brightens the eyes and removes cataracts, breaks a stagnant pregnancy to induce miscarriage, and discharges blocked blood. Also called Jujumai. Grows in river valleys.

Baijiang (Patrinia root). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for sudden fiery heat sores, red qi, scabies and itching, ulcers and hemorrhoids, saddle sores, and heat qi. Also called Luchang. Grows in river valleys.

Baizhi (Angelica dahurica root). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for women's red-and-white vaginal discharge, blood blockage, genital swelling, cold-heat, wind headache, and eye afflictions with tearing; promotes growth and moistening of flesh and skin, and can be made into a facial ointment. Also called Fangxiang. Grows in river valleys.

Duruo (Pollia japonica). Taste pungent, slightly warm. Indicated for rebellious qi below the chest and ribs; warms the middle, treats wind entering the cranial cavity with head swelling and pain, and excessive nasal mucus and tearing. Taken long-term, boosts the essence, brightens the eyes, and lightens the body. Also called Duheng. Grows in river marshes.

Zicao (Lithospermum, purple gromwell root). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for evil qi of the heart and abdomen, and the five types of jaundice; tonifies the middle and boosts qi, frees the nine orifices, and opens the waterways. Also called Zidan, also called Zifu, also called Dixue. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ziwan (Aster tataricus root). Taste bitter, warm. Indicated for cough with rebellious ascending qi, and bound cold-heat qi in the chest; removes gu-poison toxin and wilting paralysis, and calms the five viscera. Grows in mountain valleys.

Baixian (Dictamnus dasycarpus, dittany bark). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for head wind, jaundice, cough with rebellious qi, dribbling urinary difficulty, women's genital swelling and pain, and damp painful obstruction (bi) with numb, dead flesh where one cannot bend or stretch the limbs, or rise and walk. Grows in river valleys.

Weixian. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for wind-damp painful obstruction (bi), joint pain throughout the body, fright epilepsy with tongue protrusion, palpitations from malicious wind, and rat-fistula (scrofulous) swellings. Also called Mixian. Grows in river marshes.

Baiwei (Cynanchum atratum root). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for sudden wind-stroke with body heat, fullness of the limbs, confusion where one fails to recognize others, mania and delusion from evil qi, cold-heat with aching pain, and warm malaria with shivering chills that recur at fixed times. Grows in river marshes.

Xier fruit (Xanthium, cocklebur fruit). Taste sweet, warm. Indicated for wind-head cold pain, wind-damp generalized painful obstruction (bi), cramping pain of the four limbs, and malignant, dead flesh. Taken long-term, boosts qi, sharpens the ears and eyes, strengthens resolve, and lightens the body. Also called Huxi, also called Digui. Grows in river valleys.

Maogen (Imperata cylindrica root, cogongrass root). Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for taxation damage and vacuity emaciation; tonifies the middle and boosts qi, removes stasis blood, and treats blood blockage with cold-heat, and frees urination. Its shoot treats water swelling. Also called Langen, also called Rugen. Grows in mountain valleys.

Suanjiang (Physalis, ground cherry). Taste sour, neutral. Indicated for heat vexation and fullness; calms the will and boosts qi, frees urination, and for difficult labor — swallowing its fruit brings immediate delivery. Also called Cujiang. Grows in river marshes.

Yinyanghuo (Epimedium, horny goat weed). Taste pungent, cold. Indicated for impotence and injury with loss of essence, pain in the penis; frees urination, boosts qi and strength, and strengthens resolve. Also called Gangqian. Grows in mountain valleys.

Lishi (Iris lactea seed). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for cold-heat of the skin, heat qi in the stomach, and wind-cold-damp painful obstruction (bi); strengthens the sinews and bones and increases appetite. Taken long-term, lightens the body. The flowers and leaves expel white parasites. Also called Jucao, also called Sanjian, also called Shishou. Grows in river valleys.

Kuandong (Tussilago farfara, coltsfoot flower). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for cough with rebellious ascending qi, tendency to wheeze, throat obstruction, various types of fright epilepsy, and cold-heat evil qi. Also called Tuowu, Kedong, Huxu, and Tuxi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Fangji (Stephania tetrandra root). Taste pungent, neutral. Indicated for wind-cold, warm malaria, and heat qi, and various types of epilepsy; removes evil qi and frees urination and defecation. Also called Jieli. Grows in river valleys.

Nüwan. Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for wind-cold with shivering chills, cholera-like sudden turmoil with diarrhea and dysentery, intestinal rumbling that moves erratically up and down, fright epilepsy, cold-heat, and a hundred other ailments. Grows in river valleys.

Zelan (Lycopus lucidus, bugleweed). Taste sour, non-toxic. Indicated for nosebleed in nursing women, residual ailments after wind-stroke, great abdominal water swelling, edema of the body, face, and four limbs, water lodged in the joints, wounds from blades, and carbuncular swelling with suppurating sores. Also called Hulan, also called Longzao. Grows beside marshes.

Diyu (Sanguisorba officinalis, burnet root). Taste bitter, slightly cold. Indicated for women's breast pain, the seven damages, and vaginal discharge disease; stops pain, removes malignant flesh, stops sweating, and treats wounds from blades. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wangsun. Taste bitter, neutral, non-toxic. Indicated for evil qi of the five viscera, cold-damp painful obstruction (bi), aching pain of the four limbs, and cold, painful knees. Grows in river valleys.

Juechuang (Justicia procumbens). Taste salty, cold. Indicated for pain of the lower back and spine so severe one cannot lie on a bed, and difficulty bending and straightening; removes heat, and can be used as a bathing decoction. Grows in mountain valleys.
